# Part 7 — The Valve {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## Variable Resistance: The Volume Knob

- Think of a **volume knob** on a speaker — turn it to control how loud it gets
- In electronics, this is called a **potentiometer** — it adjusts resistance smoothly
- More resistance → less current → dimmer LED / slower motor

**You can find this in:**
Volume knobs · Brightness dimmers · Fan speed controls

| Position | What happens |
|----------|-------------|
| Fully turned one way | Maximum resistance → minimum current |
| Middle | Medium resistance → medium current |
| Fully the other way | Zero resistance → maximum current |

## The Valve Simulation {background-color="#f7f9fc"}

<iframe data-src="../simulations/water_circuit/micro/valve.html"
        width="100%" height="520px"
        style="border:none; border-radius:12px;"></iframe>

*Manually rotate the valve. Watch the current change in real time.*

## From Analog to Digital

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="50%"}

**Potentiometer (Analog)**

- Smooth range of values
- Like a dimmer switch — infinite steps between off and fully on

::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}

**A GPIO Pin (Digital)**

- Only two states: HIGH or LOW, ON or OFF
- Like a light switch — no in-between

::::

:::::

::: {.fragment}

> Your robot uses **both**: analog sensors (like the LDR) that give smooth values, and digital pins that decide HIGH or LOW.

:::

---

## 🔥 Bonus — The Automatic Valve: The Transistor {background-color="#1C355E" .sdaia-dark}

> For curious students: what if the *valve* could open itself?

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}

Remember the valve that controls water flow?

A **transistor** is like a **magic valve** — instead of turning it with your hand, you open it with a **tiny electrical signal**.

- 🤏 **Tiny signal in** → 💧 **Big current out**
- The bigger the signal, the more it opens
- No signal at all → it stays completely **closed**

::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import schemdraw
import schemdraw.elements as elm

with schemdraw.Drawing() as d:
    # The "Magic Valve" (Transistor)
    Q = d.add(elm.BjtNpn(reverse=True).anchor('base'))
    
    # Tiny Control Signal
    d += elm.Line().right().at(Q.base).length(0.5).label('Tiny Signal\n(Control)', loc='right')
    
    # Big Flow Path
    d += elm.Line().up().at(Q.collector).length(1).label('Big Power\n(Main Flow)', loc='top')
    d += elm.Line().down().at(Q.emitter).length(1).label('Out to Robot', loc='bottom')
    
    d.draw()
```

::::

:::::

::: {.fragment}
> 💡 Your Arduino sends a tiny signal — the transistor uses it to switch a **motor** or **LED** that needs far more power than the Arduino can provide directly.
:::

## 🔥 Bonus — Transistors = The Brain of Everything {background-color="#1C355E" .sdaia-dark}

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="50%"}

**Fully OPEN** → current flows freely

→ we call this **"1"** (ON)

**Fully CLOSED** → no current at all

→ we call this **"0"** (OFF)

::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}

| Signal | Valve | Meaning |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Strong | Open  | **1**   |
| None   | Closed| **0**   |

::::

:::::

::: {.fragment}

> 🤯 A modern computer chip has **billions** of tiny transistors — each one switching between **0** and **1** billions of times per second.
> That is how your phone runs games, videos, and apps — all from billions of tiny automatic valves!

:::
